does: This paper introduces FoeGlass, an automated black-box red-teaming method for audio deepfake detectors that leverages in-context learning to generate adversarial audio samples. The approach uses an LLM to explore the input space of text-to-speech models and craft prompts that produce audio outputs specifically designed to fool target ADD systems. A key technical innovation is the use of diversity-aware context generation to prevent mode collapse, a common failure mode in automated red-teaming where the system repeatedly discovers only similar failure cases. The method requires only black-box access to the TTS and ADD models, making it practical against deployed systems. Empirical evaluation demonstrates that FoeGlass successfully identifies blind spots in existing deepfake detectors that are not well-represented in current benchmark datasets.
connects: This work extends the growing field of adversarial red-teaming methods, which have become increasingly important for evaluating AI safety across multiple modalities. It builds on prior research in black-box adversarial attacks and automated dataset construction for robustness testing, adapting techniques from vision and NLP adversarial settings to the audio domain. The paper responds to limitations in existing audio deepfake detection benchmarks, which typically rely on manual collection and may not comprehensively cover the space of synthesized speech. It leverages recent advances in large language model in-context learning capabilities, recognizing that LLMs can be repurposed as efficient explorers of model input spaces. The work complements existing ADD evaluation frameworks and connects to broader efforts in TTS safety and detection, building on the recognition that detection models need systematic evaluation against diverse generation mechanisms.
enables: FoeGlass establishes a foundation for systematic adversarial evaluation of audio deepfake detectors that could drive improvements in ADD robustness. By enabling efficient discovery of detector blind spots without manual effort, the method can facilitate development of more comprehensive and challenging evaluation datasets that better reflect real-world deepfake threats. The approach could accelerate the iterative cycle of ADD model improvement, where developers generate challenging samples, retrain models, and repeat. More broadly, the black-box red-teaming framework demonstrates a template for adversarial testing of other audio-based AI systems and could inspire similar in-context learning approaches in other modalities. The work raises important questions about the coverage and transferability of deepfake detection across diverse TTS architectures and generation strategies, pointing toward future research on more robust and generalizable ADD methods.
